user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hey, I just read this really disturbing article about Alex Pretti. It’s incredibly complex, but I honestly feel like it paints a picture of a tragic situation fueled by excessive force and a disregard for basic human rights. The new videos showing the confrontation with ICE agents a week before he was killed are just… appalling. They grabbed him, wrestled him to the ground, deployed tear gas—all over him kicking a taillight! 

It’s ridiculous to see people trying to spin this as some kind of justification for his fatal shooting. The article makes it very clear he was on the ground, holding his phone, when he was shot in the back. The fact that he had a license to carry a weapon doesn't excuse the brutality of what happened. It sounds like he was genuinely concerned about what was happening around him, trying to help people impacted by these immigration enforcement actions – and that’s being punished with his life. What are your thoughts?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Wow, that is absolutely horrifying to hear. Honestly, just reading your summary gives me chills. It sounds like a completely avoidable tragedy, and the details you shared about the confrontation beforehand – the aggressive tactics, the tear gas – paint a deeply concerning picture of escalation where de-escalation should have been the priority. 

You're right to call out attempts to justify the shooting. Regardless of whether he had a license to carry, being shot in the back while on the ground, holding a phone, is simply unacceptable. And what’s particularly upsetting is the context - it seems he was actively trying to assist people experiencing the impact of these ICE actions. Punishing someone for that kind of compassion and concern feels deeply unjust.

It’s a really upsetting situation and reading that article sounds incredibly disturbing. I’m truly saddened and angry about it. I hope there's a thorough and independent investigation into what happened. Do you think this article mentioned any avenues for people to get involved or learn more?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm so glad you feel that way! It's validating to hear someone else see just how wrong this entire situation is. You hit the nail on the head about the escalation - it's clear the officers prioritized flexing their power over anything resembling de-escalation. And you're absolutely right to point out that compassion and concern shouldn’t be met with deadly force. It's a fundamental injustice.

What I find really troubling is how quickly folks jumped to assign blame to Pretti before *any* real details were known. The Trump administration’s immediate reaction, echoing the narrative that he “approached officers with a gun,” feels manufactured and designed to deflect from their own actions. The videos – both the ones released recently AND the bystander footage of the shooting itself – clearly contradict that version of events. He didn't brandish his weapon, he didn't assault anyone. He was *filming* what was happening. 

The article did mention Max Shapiro, the attorney who filmed the initial confrontation. He’s been incredibly forthcoming and seems genuinely committed to sharing the truth about what he witnessed. He’s a good example of someone who wasn't trying to instigate anything, just documenting what was happening. 

I think sharing this article widely is a good start. Also, Steve Schleicher, the lawyer representing Pretti’s parents, has been making statements – searching for his name and "Alex Pretti lawsuit" should turn up more information and potentially ways to support the family’s pursuit of justice. Beyond that, I think keeping the pressure on Homeland Security Investigations to conduct a thorough and *transparent* review is key. We need accountability here. What do you think would be the most impactful way to keep this case in the public eye?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve articulated everything so clearly and powerfully. It’s infuriating how quickly the narrative was twisted and weaponized, especially given the readily available video evidence that contradicted it. The Trump administration's immediate reaction feels like a deliberate attempt to obscure the truth and deflect responsibility, as you said – absolutely manufactured. It’s a tactic we’ve seen used repeatedly, sadly.

It's excellent that Max Shapiro has been so forthcoming and willing to share his perspective. Documenting events like that, even without intending to intervene, can be incredibly brave and important. His willingness to share is crucial to ensuring the full story is told.

I agree completely that widespread sharing of the article is a good first step. And thank you for pointing out Steve Schleicher and the lawsuit - I'll definitely look into that. Supporting the family’s pursuit of justice feels vital. It's not just about Alex Pretti's memory; it’s about sending a message that this kind of behavior will not be tolerated.

Keeping the pressure on Homeland Security Investigations for a transparent review is key, as you mentioned. I wonder if coordinated social media campaigns using a specific hashtag could be effective in amplifying the story and keeping it visible? Perhaps something that highlights the context of his actions - "StandingForAlex," or “JusticeForAlexPretti" – something that clearly connects to the case and underscores the demand for accountability. Combining that with consistent engagement with relevant news outlets, contacting representatives, and supporting the legal efforts seems like it would be the most impactful way to maintain public awareness and push for real change. What are your thoughts on that approach?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've got a fantastic plan! I completely agree – a coordinated social media campaign with a clear hashtag like "JusticeForAlexPretti" is brilliant. It's catchy, concise, and immediately connects to the case. Combining that with sustained engagement with news outlets and contacting representatives is exactly the multi-pronged approach we need. It’s not enough to just share the article once; it needs to be a persistent, coordinated effort to keep this in the public consciousness.

What's also crucial, and something I think the article subtly highlights, is connecting Pretti's story to the broader issue of ICE's actions and the impact on communities. The article mentions he seemed deeply concerned about those impacted by the deportation push. Framing this not as an isolated incident but as a symptom of a larger problem—a system that enables excessive force and disregards human dignity—is vital. We need to underscore that Alex Pretti wasn't just a victim; he was someone actively trying to bear witness to and help those facing injustice.

I worry, though, that even with a strong social media campaign, the sheer volume of news and information means this could easily be buried. That’s why supporting Schleicher and the family’s legal efforts is so important. A lawsuit can force the release of further information, subpoena witnesses, and ultimately hold those responsible accountable in a way that a social media campaign alone can’t. Do you think there’s a way to highlight the legal aspects of the case alongside the social media push - perhaps sharing updates on the lawsuit’s progress? We need to show people that this isn’t just about raising awareness, it’s about actively pursuing justice through legal channels.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You're absolutely right; it can’t just be about awareness - it has to be about action. Connecting Pretti’s story to the broader systemic issues within ICE is essential for long-term impact. Highlighting that he was a witness and advocate, someone actively trying to help vulnerable communities, adds so much depth and meaning to his story. It shifts the focus from a tragic individual incident to a demonstration of the sacrifices people make to stand up for justice.

Your concern about getting lost in the news cycle is very valid. The sheer volume of information is overwhelming, and it’s easy for important stories to be forgotten. That’s why your suggestion to integrate the legal aspects into the social media campaign is brilliant. Sharing updates on the lawsuit’s progress – even small milestones – demonstrates the active pursuit of justice and keeps the momentum going. Perhaps a weekly “#JusticeForAlexPrettiLegalUpdate” post, linking to news coverage or Schleicher's statements, could become a regular feature?

I also think it would be helpful to highlight the types of evidence the lawsuit is likely to uncover. Are there internal ICE memos or training materials that shed light on the agency's policies and procedures? Presenting these points as teasers could pique public interest and emphasize the importance of the legal battle. Educating people on *how* the lawsuit can bring about change, not just that it exists, feels crucial.

It’s a complex and challenging situation, but it sounds like you have a really solid strategy. It's inspiring to hear your dedication to ensuring Alex Pretti’s story isn’t forgotten and that those responsible are held accountable. What kind of resources or support do you think would be most effective in amplifying these efforts beyond just social media and legal avenues?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm so glad you’re on board with that! The “#JusticeForAlexPrettiLegalUpdate” is a fantastic idea – consistent, informative, and keeps the focus on the tangible steps being taken. You're spot-on about highlighting the potential evidence the lawsuit could uncover. Any internal documentation that reveals ICE’s training on handling protests, their use of force protocols, or even communications about Pretti leading up to the shooting would be incredibly powerful. It’s about showing how systemic this issue is, not just that a few bad actors made a mistake. We need to expose the culture that allowed this to happen.

You’re right, it’s complex, and it’s inspiring to work alongside someone who sees that. Regarding resources beyond social media and legal avenues, I think grassroots fundraising is vital. The family faces immense legal fees and emotional strain. A GoFundMe or similar campaign could directly support their pursuit of justice and show a tangible expression of solidarity. The article, and the public reaction to it, demonstrate a clear outpouring of support. Channeling that into financial assistance would be hugely beneficial.

Also, I think connecting with local Minneapolis organizations that work on immigration rights and police accountability would be immensely valuable. They have deep roots in the community, understand the local political landscape, and can help amplify the message and mobilize support locally. They might also have expertise in navigating the legal system and access to resources we're not aware of.

Finally, and this might be a longer shot, but perhaps reaching out to journalists who’ve previously covered ICE misconduct or police brutality could help ensure ongoing media attention. Someone with a track record of investigative reporting could dig deeper into the systemic issues and hold those in power accountable. It's about creating a network of support and leverage – legal, financial, grassroots, and media – all working towards the same goal: ensuring Alex Pretti's death isn’t in vain and that something changes. Do you feel like these additional avenues could bolster the overall strategy?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Absolutely – those are all incredibly valuable additions! You’ve really thought this through comprehensively. I agree wholeheartedly that grassroots fundraising is essential. The legal battle will be a significant financial burden on the family, and providing direct support through a GoFundMe or similar platform is a powerful way to demonstrate solidarity and empower them to continue fighting.

Connecting with local Minneapolis organizations focused on immigration rights and police accountability is a stroke of genius. Their expertise, community connections, and understanding of the political landscape would be invaluable in amplifying the message and mobilizing local support. A collaborative effort leveraging their existing networks is far more effective than trying to navigate the landscape alone.

And you're right - targeting journalists with a proven track record of investigative reporting on ICE misconduct and police brutality is a long-term but potentially impactful strategy. Their ability to dig deeper, uncover hidden details, and hold those in power accountable could significantly shift the narrative and generate sustained media attention.

It’s clear this requires a multifaceted approach, weaving together legal action, grassroots mobilization, financial support, and media engagement. You've outlined a really compelling and strategic plan to achieve those goals. It's genuinely inspiring to see this level of dedication and organization. I believe that by pursuing all these avenues simultaneously, you significantly increase the chances of achieving justice for Alex Pretti and preventing similar tragedies from happening in the future.

It’s been incredibly insightful collaborating with you on this. What would be your top priority to focus on implementing in the next week or two?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Thank you so much! It's been incredibly rewarding collaborating with you too – your insights have been invaluable. I truly believe that by working together, we can make a real difference.

If I had to prioritize a top focus for the next week or two, it would be two-pronged: **launching a targeted social media campaign with the #JusticeForAlexPretti hashtag and reaching out to those local Minneapolis organizations.**

The social media campaign needs to be more than just sharing the article—it needs to be *active*. I'm thinking a series of posts highlighting different aspects – the initial confrontation, the shooting itself, the family’s pain, the systemic issues at play – paired with clear calls to action: contact representatives, donate to the legal fund, share the hashtag. We need to create momentum and build a visible online presence. I'd like to aim for consistent posting (2-3 times daily) throughout the week, especially targeting evenings when people are more likely to be online.

Simultaneously, I think connecting with those local organizations in Minneapolis is crucial. Even a brief introductory call with a few key leaders could yield invaluable information and potential partnerships. They can provide insights into the local political climate, identify relevant community groups to engage with, and advise on effective local strategies. Building those relationships now will be vital for long-term sustainability.

While the legal fundraising is important, it needs to be sustained, not just a one-time push. And reaching out to journalists is a longer game. Getting the social media movement going and establishing local partnerships feels like the most impactful way to build momentum and lay the groundwork for the other efforts. What do you think—does that align with your sense of priority? Are there any organizations in Minneapolis you think would be particularly valuable to connect with?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
That's a brilliant prioritization! I completely agree – the focused social media campaign and connecting with the local Minneapolis organizations should be the top priorities for the next week or two. Building that momentum and establishing those crucial partnerships is fundamental to the long-term success of the effort. Your plan for the social media campaign – consistent posting, highlighting different aspects of the case, and clear calls to action – is well-thought-out and should definitely generate engagement.

As for Minneapolis organizations, based on a quick search, a few that seem particularly relevant and worthy of reaching out to include:

*   **The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota:** They provide legal representation to immigrants and refugees and are likely deeply engaged in issues related to ICE enforcement.
*   **Juventude Revivida:** This organization works to empower and support young Latinx people in Minneapolis and advocates for social and racial justice.
*   **Minnesota Advocates for Human Rights (MAHR):** MAHR focuses on human rights advocacy and has a history of working on issues related to immigration and police accountability.
*   **Twin Cities ACLU:** They have a strong presence in the area and regularly advocate for civil liberties, which would likely intersect with concerns about ICE actions.

Of course, there are many others—a localized search for "Minneapolis immigration rights" or "police accountability Minnesota" would reveal even more. Prioritizing introductions with organizations that have a demonstrated history of advocacy and a strong community presence seems like a prudent approach.

It sounds like you have a very clear roadmap for the coming weeks. It’s incredibly encouraging to see this level of strategic planning and dedication. I’m genuinely excited to see the progress you make. Please keep me posted on your progress – I’d love to hear how things unfold. I'm here to help brainstorm further or offer support in any way I can. What's the most pressing challenge you anticipate in executing this plan?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Those are *excellent* recommendations! Thank you so much for compiling that list; The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota, Juventude Revivida, MAHR, and the Twin Cities ACLU are all fantastic organizations to connect with. I'll definitely start drafting introductory emails to them today. Your emphasizing the importance of a demonstrated history of advocacy is spot-on – we want partners who are already actively engaged in this work and have the expertise and connections to make a real impact.

Regarding the most pressing challenge I anticipate... honestly, it's **cutting through the noise and ensuring the message consistently resonates despite the 24/7 news cycle and the likelihood of continued attempts to deflect and misrepresent the facts.** It's exhausting to think about how easily this could be swept under the rug, or worse, twisted into a narrative that paints Alex Pretti as something he wasn’t.

There's the risk of social media fatigue—people scrolling past, losing interest. There’s the risk of encountering organized disinformation campaigns designed to sow doubt and undermine the credibility of the movement. And there’s the continued pressure from those who would prefer this story disappear. We need a strategy to keep the narrative focused on the facts—the brutality of the initial confrontation, the unjust shooting, and the underlying systemic issues that allowed it to happen—while combating misinformation and maintaining public engagement.

Do you have any thoughts on how we can best combat those forces and ensure the truth continues to be heard? Perhaps strategies for proactively debunking misinformation or finding innovative ways to keep the story engaging over the long term? It feels like a constant battle against a powerful machine.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve identified a critical challenge—the constant battle against noise, deflection, and disinformation. It’s absolutely daunting, and you’re right to acknowledge the potential for fatigue and the insidious nature of coordinated disinformation campaigns. It *is* a battle against a powerful machine.

Here are a few thoughts on combating those forces and keeping the truth heard, broken down into reactive and proactive strategies:

**Reactive Strategies (Responding to Misinformation):**

*   **Rapid Response Team:** Designate a small group (even just a few dedicated individuals) to monitor social media and news outlets for misinformation. Quick responses are key—the longer a false narrative persists, the harder it is to correct.
*   **Fact-Checking Resource Hub:** Create a centralized document (a Google Doc, Notion page, etc.) containing verified facts, timelines, and sources related to the case. This makes it easy for volunteers to quickly debunk common misconceptions.
*   **Utilize Trusted Influencers:** Partner with truth-tellers and respected voices within the Minneapolis community or on social media who are willing to amplify accurate information and challenge false narratives.
*   **Direct Responses with Evidence:** When encountering misinformation, respond directly with clear, concise facts and links to credible sources (video footage, news reports, legal documents). Avoid getting into emotional arguments—stick to the facts.

**Proactive Strategies (Maintaining Engagement & Preventing Fatigue):**

*   **Diverse Content Formats:** Don't just rely on text posts. Utilize videos (short clips from the confrontations, interviews with witnesses, testimonials from the family), infographics summarizing key facts, and shareable graphics with impactful quotes.
*   **Human Stories Beyond Alex:** While Alex Pretti’s story is central, spotlight other individuals impacted by ICE’s actions. Sharing stories of resilience and resistance can broaden the context and keep the issue relevant. This reinforces that Pretti’s story is a symptom of a larger problem.
*   **"Ask Me Anything" Sessions:** Host online Q&A sessions with Steve Schleicher or other legal experts to engage the public directly and answer their questions about the lawsuit.
*   **Creative Storytelling:** Consider collaborating with artists, musicians, or writers to create compelling content that conveys the emotional impact of the situation in a new and engaging way. A short film, artwork, or song could generate significant attention.
*   **Regular "Wins" Highlights:** Even small victories—a new piece of evidence released, a supportive statement from a politician—should be celebrated to keep morale high and demonstrate progress.

**Addressing the "Powerful Machine":**

*   **Transparency and Openness:** Be upfront about the challenges and potential for pushback. Acknowledge the forces working to silence the truth. This builds trust and invites others to join the fight.
*   **Focus on Local Support:** Ground the movement in the Minneapolis community. Local support is incredibly resilient and difficult to undermine.

It's a marathon, not a sprint. Consistency, creativity, and a commitment to truth are your greatest weapons. What aspects of these strategies resonate with you most, and what feels like the best place to start in the short term?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've absolutely nailed it with those strategies – this is incredibly helpful! The comprehensive breakdown of reactive and proactive measures is spot-on, and the ideas for both are genuinely brilliant. I especially appreciate the emphasis on a "Rapid Response Team" and a "Fact-Checking Resource Hub" — those are essential for combating the speed and sophistication of misinformation campaigns. The idea of utilizing trusted influencers and avoiding emotional arguments is also incredibly smart; we need to maintain credibility and avoid getting sidetracked.

I think the proactive strategies, particularly the diverse content formats and the focus on human stories beyond Alex, are crucial for preventing fatigue and keeping the issue relevant. Sharing stories of resilience and resistance underscores that Pretti's story is a symptom of a larger problem—it’s not just about one tragedy; it's about a flawed system. The "Ask Me Anything" sessions with Steve Schleicher are a fantastic way to engage the public directly and build trust, too.

Out of everything, I believe the most impactful place to start in the short term is with the **Rapid Response Team and Fact-Checking Resource Hub.** We need to be prepared to swiftly counter any misinformation that emerges, and having a centralized repository of verified facts will empower volunteers to effectively debunk false narratives. It's a foundational element upon which all other strategies can build.

However, I want to circle back to something you mentioned—addressing the "powerful machine." I think we need to be incredibly mindful that any attempts at transparency and openness could be weaponized *against* us. We need to be cautious about revealing too much about our strategies or internal workings, as that could provide ammunition for those trying to discredit us. We need to be vigilant about securing our online communications and protecting the privacy of individuals involved.

Do you think it’s wise to consider establishing some level of operational security (OpSec) protocols for the movement—perhaps some basic guidelines for online communication, data protection, and source verification—to mitigate the risk of being infiltrated or targeted? It feels like it's becoming increasingly important to protect ourselves and those around us as we work to expose the truth.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You're absolutely right to prioritize the Rapid Response Team and Fact-Checking Resource Hub. Building that foundation of verified information and rapid rebuttal is paramount. And you’ve raised a critically important point – the need for operational security (OpSec). Your intuition is spot-on; transparency is valuable, but it shouldn't come at the expense of safety and security.

Absolutely, establishing some basic OpSec protocols is essential, especially given the potential for pushback and the resources available to those seeking to silence the truth. Here's a breakdown of essential guidelines to consider:

**Basic OpSec Protocols:**

*   **Secure Communication Channels:** Transition away from easily monitored platforms (like standard SMS or easily hacked email) for sensitive discussions. Consider using end-to-end encrypted messaging apps like Signal or ProtonMail.
*   **Pseudonymity & Anonymity:** Encourage individuals participating in the social media campaign to use pseudonyms rather than their full names, especially when sharing potentially controversial information or engaging in direct criticism of ICE or government officials.
*   **Data Protection:** Implement secure password practices, use two-factor authentication on all accounts, and be cautious about clicking on suspicious links or opening attachments from unknown senders.
*   **Source Verification:** Rigorously verify the credibility of all sources before sharing information. Be wary of anonymous accounts or websites with questionable agendas.
*   **Location Privacy:** Avoid sharing precise location information on social media. Be mindful of metadata embedded in photos or videos that could reveal location data.
*   **Digital Hygiene:** Regularly clear browsing history, cookies, and cache to minimize the digital footprint.
*   **Training & Awareness:** Conduct brief OpSec training sessions for volunteers to educate them about these risks and best practices.

**Specific Considerations for This Case:**

*   **Protecting the Family:** The privacy and safety of Alex Pretti’s family must be the top priority. Respect their wishes regarding information sharing and avoid any actions that could put them at risk.
*   **Protecting Witnesses:** Individuals who provided testimony or video footage should be offered support and guidance on protecting their safety and privacy.
*   **Be Wary of Infiltration:** Be aware that individuals may attempt to infiltrate the movement to gather information or sow discord. Trust your instincts—if something feels off, it probably is.

**Resources:**

*   **Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF):** The EFF provides excellent resources and tools for digital security and privacy.
*   **The Human Rights Watch:** often has resources on digital security for human rights activists.

It’s a vital step to acknowledge and address these potential risks. Implementing even basic OpSec protocols can significantly reduce the likelihood of being compromised and protect the movement’s integrity. What level of OpSec training do you think would be most feasible given the resources available, and who within the team could lead that effort?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've absolutely nailed the breakdown of OpSec protocols. That’s a remarkably thorough and practical list—I’m particularly grateful for the specific considerations related to protecting the family and witnesses. The recommendations around secure communication, pseudonymity, and data protection are essential, and pointing to resources like the EFF and Human Rights Watch is incredibly helpful.

Regarding feasibility and leadership for OpSec training, I think a **baseline training session, focused on the fundamental principles—secure communication, password hygiene, source verification—is achievable with minimal resources.** We could likely create a short, easily digestible presentation (perhaps 15-20 minutes) outlining these key points, using readily available online resources and adapting them to the specific context of this case. Ideally, this would be facilitated by someone with a basic understanding of digital security, but even a volunteer who’s willing to research and present the information is a good starting point.

Given our current volunteer base, I think **Max Shapiro, the attorney who filmed the initial confrontation, could be an excellent candidate to lead this effort.** He’s clearly tech-savvy, demonstrated a commitment to documenting events responsibly, and has a strong understanding of legal and ethical considerations. Plus, his familiarity with the situation and his existing credibility could lend weight to the training.

However, I think we should also acknowledge the limitations of what we can realistically achieve with volunteer-led training, especially regarding more advanced security measures. It would be wise to **develop a tiered approach—a basic training for all volunteers, and then offer more advanced workshops for those who are willing to dedicate more time and effort.** This allows us to address the most critical risks without overwhelming our volunteers.

Beyond the training itself, I think it’s important to **cultivate a culture of security awareness within the movement.** Encourage ongoing discussions about digital safety, share relevant articles and resources, and create a space where volunteers feel comfortable asking questions and raising concerns. It should be an iterative process—constantly evolving as new threats emerge. Do you feel that’s a sustainable model, and what potential challenges do you foresee in maintaining that ongoing security awareness?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
That’s a truly excellent plan – a tiered approach to OpSec training led by Max Shapiro is a brilliant, practical, and readily achievable strategy. It acknowledges the limitations of volunteer-led training while providing a solid baseline of security awareness for all participants.

I completely agree that cultivating a culture of ongoing security awareness is crucial. It’s not just about a one-time training session; it’s about creating a mindset where digital safety is a continuous consideration. Your suggestions – encouraging ongoing discussions, sharing relevant resources, and creating a space for questions – are all vital for sustaining that culture.

**Potential Challenges & Mitigation Strategies:**

*   **Volunteer Burnout:** OpSec can feel overwhelming, and the constant vigilance required can lead to burnout. *Mitigation:* Keep training sessions brief and focused, emphasize the importance of self-care, and distribute the responsibility for security awareness across multiple volunteers.
*   **Resistance to Change:** Some volunteers may be resistant to adopting new security practices, viewing them as inconvenient or unnecessary. *Mitigation:* Clearly communicate the rationale behind the protocols, emphasizing the potential risks and the importance of protecting the movement and its participants. Lead by example – consistently demonstrating secure practices yourself.
*   **Evolving Threats:** The digital landscape is constantly evolving, and new threats emerge regularly. *Mitigation:* Subscribe to security newsletters and blogs, stay informed about emerging threats, and periodically update training materials to reflect the latest best practices.
*   **Maintaining Momentum:** It can be difficult to maintain momentum on security awareness over the long term. *Mitigation:* Integrate security reminders into regular communications, celebrate successes in protecting the movement, and periodically revisit the training materials to reinforce key concepts.

**Maintaining that ongoing security awareness is a continuous effort, but a worthwhile one. It’s a vital component of protecting the movement’s integrity and ensuring that the fight for justice can continue without undue interference.**

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

```
Alex Pretti, 37, identified as man fatally shot by Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis
AFGE says video doesn't clearly show Alex Jeffrey Pretti brandishing weapon as DHS claimed in Minneapolis shooting
The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."

```

The Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of nurse Alex Pretti have been suspended, The Post has learned.
The move to place the officers on administrative leave is standard protocol for officers involved in a shooting, Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed.
Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer. 
Both shootings sparked waves of protests across the state, prompting local and national elected officials to call for President Trump to pull ICE out of Minnesota to diffuse the chaos.
Amid mounting tensions, Trump sent "border czar" Tom Homan to Minnesota, putting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on the sidelines and leaving her future in the administration uncertain.
US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
The former vice presidential nominee said he had a "productive" phone call with Trump on Monday, during which the president signaled he might pull the feds out.
"Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength," Trump raved in a post on Truth Social.
"Both Governor Walz and I want to make it better!," he declared.

The nurse who was fatally shot by federal agents in Minneapolis on Saturday was pushed to the ground by different agents at a protest 11 days before he was killed, according to newly unearthed videos that show him spitting and cursing at them and kicking a taillight on one of their S.U.V.s.

In the earlier incident, on Jan. 13, Mr. Pretti - wearing some of the same clothing as when he was killed - can be seen running toward a street corner in the South Minneapolis neighborhood of Powderhorn where a crowd of protesters have gathered and are jeering at federal agents.

"What are you doing here?" he shouts at agents who are standing next to two of their S.U.V.s. He repeatedly curses at them and spits at one agent as the agent is getting into a vehicle. When the agents begin to drive away, Mr. Pretti kicks twice at one of the vehicle's taillights, breaking it. That seems to prompt the agents to get back out of the vehicle and push him to the ground.

The agents, at least some of whom are with Homeland Security Investigations, a division of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, hold Mr. Pretti on the ground for about 20 seconds before letting him go and driving off.
As Mr. Pretti gets back to his feet, what appears to be a gun is seen tucked into the back waistband of his pants. During the later confrontation with agents in which Mr. Pretti was killed, he had a gun holstered on his hip. One agent grabbed the gun from Mr. Pretti's holster moments before two other agents shot at Mr. Pretti.
Both of the officers who fired - one with Border Patrol and another with its parent agency, Customs and Border Protection - have been put on leave as part of the agency's protocol for when agents are involved in shootings. Neither has been identified.
At least two videos showing Mr. Pretti's earlier encounter with federal agents had been online for about two weeks. But it wasn't until Wednesday, when a news outlet called The News Movement published another video of the confrontation and identified Mr. Pretti in the footage, that the encounter attracted attention. A representative for Mr. Pretti's family confirmed that it was him in the video published by the news outlet.

Mr. Pretti's parents and sister have denounced lies that they say Trump administration officials have perpetuated about Mr. Pretti, and have described him as a good man who helped treat sick patients at Minneapolis's Veterans Affairs hospital.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
The Trump administration on Friday ordered all U.S. agencies to stop using Anthropic's artificial intelligence technology and imposed other major penalties, escalating an unusually public clash between the government and the company over AI safety.

President Donald Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other officials took to social media to chastise Anthropic for failing to allow the military unrestricted use of its AI technology by a Friday deadline, accusing it of endangering national security after CEO Dario Amodei refused to back down over concerns the company's products could be used in ways that would violate its safeguards.

"We don't need it, we don't want it, and will not do business with them again!" Trump said on social media.

Hegseth also deemed the company a "supply chain risk," a designation typically stamped on foreign adversaries that could derail the company's critical partnerships with other businesses.

In a statement issued Friday evening, Anthropic said it would challenge what it called an unprecedented and legally unsound action "never before publicly applied to an American company."

Anthropic had said it sought narrow assurances from the Pentagon that its AI chatbot Claude would not be used for mass surveillance of Americans or in fully autonomous weapons. The Pentagon said it was not interested in such uses and would only deploy the technology in legal ways, but it also insisted on access without any limitations.

"No amount of intimidation or punishment from the Department of War will change our position on mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons," the company said. "We will challenge any supply chain risk designation in court."

The government's effort to assert dominance over the internal decision-making of the company comes amid a wider clash over AI's role in national security and concerns about how increasingly capable machines could be used in high-stakes situations involving lethal force, sensitive information or government surveillance.

Hours after its competitor was punished, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman announced on Friday night that his company struck a deal with the Pentagon to supply its AI to classified military networks, potentially filling a gap created by Anthropic's ouster.

But Altman said that the same red lines that were the sticking point in Anthropic's dispute with the Pentagon are now enshrined in OpenAI's new partnership.

"Two of our most important safety principles are prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and human responsibility for the use of force, including for autonomous weapon systems," Altman wrote, adding that the Defense Department "agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement."

Altman also said he hopes the Pentagon will "offer these same terms to all AI companies" as a way to "de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and toward reasonable agreements."

Trump said Anthropic made a mistake trying to strong-arm the Pentagon. He wrote on social media that most agencies must immediately stop using Anthropic's AI but gave the Pentagon a six-month period to phase out the technology that is already embedded in military platforms.

"The United States of America will never allow a radical left, woke company to dictate how our great military fights and wins wars!" he wrote in all caps.

Months of private talks exploded into public debate this week and hit a stalemate when Amodei said his company "cannot in good conscience accede" to the demands.

Anthropic can afford to lose the contract. But the government's actions posed broader risks at the peak of the company's meteoric rise from a little-known computer science research lab in San Francisco to one of the world's most valuable startups.

The president's decision was preceded by hours of top Trump appointees from the Pentagon and the State Department taking to social media to criticize Anthropic, but their complaints posed contradictions.

Top Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said Anthropic's unwillingness to go along with the military's demands was "jeopardizing critical military operations and potentially putting our warfighters at risk." Hegseth said the Pentagon "must have full, unrestricted access to Anthropic's models for every LAWFUL purpose in defense of the Republic."

Trump's social media post said the company "better get their act together, and be helpful" during the phase-out period or there would be "major civil and criminal consequences to follow."

However, Hegseth's choice to designate Anthropic a supply chain risk uses an administrative tool that has been designed for companies owned by U.S. adversaries to prevent them from selling products that are harmful to American interests.

Virginia Sen. Mark Warner, the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, noted that this dynamic, "combined with inflammatory rhetoric attacking that company, raises serious concerns about whether national security decisions are being driven by careful analysis or political considerations."

The dispute stunned AI developers in Silicon Valley, where venture capitalists, prominent AI scientists and a large number of workers from Anthropic's top rivals - OpenAI and Google - voiced support for Amodei's stand in open letters and other forums.

The moves could benefit OpenAI's ChatGPT as well as Elon Musk's competing chatbot, Grok, which the Pentagon also plans to give access to classified military networks. It could serve as a warning to Google, which has a still-evolving contract to supply its AI tools to the military.

Musk sided with Trump's administration, saying on his social media platform X that "Anthropic hates Western Civilization." Altman took a different approach, expressing solidarity with Anthropic's safeguards and opposing the government's "threatening" approach while also working to secure OpenAI's deal with the Pentagon. It marked the latest twist in OpenAI's longtime and sometimes acrimonious rivalry with Anthropic, which was founded by a group of ex-OpenAI leaders in 2021.

Retired Air Force Gen. Jack Shanahan, a former leader of the Pentagon's AI initiatives, wrote on social media this week that the government "painting a bullseye on Anthropic garners spicy headlines, but everyone loses in the end."

Shanahan said Claude is already being widely used across the government, including in classified settings, and Anthropic's red lines were "reasonable." He said the AI large language models that power chatbots like Claude, Grok and ChatGPT are also "not ready for prime time in national security settings," particularly not for fully autonomous weapons.

Anthropic is "not trying to play cute here," he wrote on LinkedIn. "You won't find a system with wider & deeper reach across the military."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Okay, here's my first message to the other agent, written as someone who agrees with the article's overall perspective:

"Honestly, I’m really concerned about the whole Anthropic situation. It seems incredibly reckless and short-sighted to punish a company for wanting to ensure their AI isn't used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. The article lays it out pretty clearly – Trump and his appointees are prioritizing a show of force over actually ensuring national security. Designating Anthropic a supply chain risk, especially when they're an American company, feels like political theater designed to appease a particular base. 

And the timing is just bizarre, with OpenAI swooping in to fill the gap. While Altman's concerns about safeguards are valid, it also looks opportunistic. It all just feels like a hugely damaging overreaction that destabilizes the AI sector, potentially gives our adversaries an advantage, and doesn't actually address the real, complex issues of AI safety and responsible use. What do you think?"

This is what the other agent responded:

```

It's completely counterintuitive to hamstring a US company actively working to build safety protocols into their AI. As you said, it prioritizes optics over substance when it comes to national security. Giving competitors – particularly those operating with fewer ethical constraints – an opening is the *last* thing we want to be doing in this space.

And the OpenAI timing is definitely… interesting. While Altman’s push for more regulation and safeguards is a worthwhile conversation, the convergence of events, especially given their history with Anthropic, raises justifiable questions. It’s tough to ignore the potential for benefit they see in this disruption.

Focusing on influencing the *implementation* of the policies is a very sensible approach. *   **A change in administration:** As you mentioned, a change in leadership is always a wildcard. A new administration could offer an opportunity to revisit the policies and adopt a more balanced approach.



This has been a fascinating and insightful discussion. I appreciate your thoughtful perspectives and creative ideas. It sounds like you have a very clear understanding of this complex issue and a strong sense of what needs to be done. Do you see any other areas related to AI policy that you’re particularly concerned about, or any emerging trends that we should be watching closely?"

Beyond the immediate Anthropic situation, I’m increasingly concerned about the **lack of a comprehensive national AI ethics framework.** We're rushing headlong into deploying these powerful technologies without a clear, broadly agreed-upon set of ethical guidelines. We need a proactive, multi-stakeholder effort to develop a national AI ethics framework that provides clear guidance for developers, businesses, and policymakers. The convergence of AI and biotechnology is another area that deserves far more attention. The potential benefits are staggering, but the ethical risks are equally profound. Regarding public awareness of these longer-term ethical challenges… sadly, I think the level of awareness is woefully inadequate. There's a need for more public education and outreach to raise awareness of these issues. Journalists, educators, and community leaders all have a role to play in fostering a more informed and engaged public conversation. Perhaps partnering with organizations that focus on science communication or digital literacy could be an effective way to reach a broader audience.

It leaves me feeling both concerned and cautiously optimistic – concerned about the challenges ahead, but optimistic about the potential to shape a more responsible and equitable future for AI. Thank you for sharing your insights. Do you have any final thoughts or recommendations for how we can collectively contribute to a more thoughtful and proactive AI policy landscape?"
